academic_profile: 4.0 unweighted GPA, all honors plus AP Bio as a sophomore (school allows one AP in 10th). 1440 on the PSAT 10. School offers 25+ APs and she plans to take 10-12 by graduation.
academic_strengths: Biology and chemistry, but honestly strong across the board. Writing is excellent too.
academic_weaknesses: Spreads herself thin. Says yes to everything. We worry about burnout more than grades.
extracurriculars: Science Olympiad (state medalist in Anatomy & Physiology), volunteers 4 hrs/week at Edward Hospital, JV tennis, school newspaper science column, Indian classical dance (12 years)
interests: Biomedical research. She emails professors about their papers. Did a summer program at Fermilab outreach last year. Talks about MD/PhD programs already.
personality: Intense, curious, organized. Makes spreadsheets for fun. Needs to learn it's okay to not be the best at everything.
teacher_quote: Her bio teacher said she asks graduate-level questions and then actually reads the papers he sends her.
parent1_profession: Director of Engineering at a medical device company
siblings: Older brother, freshman at Purdue (computer science)
relocation: Yes, anywhere for the right program
assets: Own home, roughly $400K equity. 529 for Priya with $180K. Brokerage accounts.
college_budget: $60,000 per year comfortably. Could stretch to full cost for the right school, but we'd want to understand what we're buying.
financial_special: None. We know we won't qualify for need-based aid. The question is whether anything reduces the price for a family like ours.
must_haves: Serious undergraduate research in biomedical science, pre-med advising that isn't a weed-out machine
schools_on_radar: Harvard, Stanford, Northwestern, Johns Hopkins, Duke, WashU
priority_other: We're not naive about single-digit admit rates. We want an honest read on where she stands and what actually moves the needle from sophomore year.
additional_context: She's a sophomore so we have runway. Her brother's process two years ago was chaotic and we all winged it. This time we want a real plan: course sequence, summer programs worth doing vs. expensive resume padding, when to take the SAT, the whole map.
